summary: DHH examines how people transform subjective observations into objective-sounding attributes, calling this pattern 'observational transformations.' He argues that statements like 'he is so weird' disguise personal interpretations as inherent facts about a person. These transformations are harmful because they reduce complex people to single defining traits, discourage further investigation, and spread virally without their original context. He recommends distinguishing between behavioral/appearance attributes (subjective) and inherent attributes (objective) before making such claims.
key_points: Subjective observations are routinely disguised as objective attributes without acknowledging the transformation
These transformations reduce people to single defining traits, closing off further inquiry into their complexity
Defining attributes spread virally and lose connection to whatever reasoning originally supported them
Behavioral and appearance attributes are inherently subjective and should not be treated as objective definitions of a person
money_quotes: The author transforms a subjective observation into a objective attribute, but neglects to make the transformation explicit and fails to argue its validity.

Out of the infinite number of attributes that belong to a single person, only one (or a few) gets to represent all the others, which probably doesn't do the rest justice and hence acts as a misrepresentation instead of a definition.
With the defining attribute(s) identified, it's quickly deemed irrelevant to investigate further, which places everything but the most apparent attributes at a grave disadvantage. Subtlety loses.
Defining attributes are so attractive that they don't need to be delivered with much persuasion to spread quickly.
Once spread, the link to the original author is lost and along with it access to the implicit arguments that formed basis for the transformation.
key_insight: When we describe people with statements like 'he is weird,' we smuggle subjective interpretations into the language of objective fact, reducing complex individuals to simplistic labels that spread unchallenged.
